title: Anthropic resets Claude Code usage limits after a week of outages
subtitle: Every Pro, Max, Team, and seat-based Enterprise user got a clean 5-hour and weekly quota on July 10 — the third such reset in seven days, and a signal that the paid tier is straining under the Claude Cowork rollout.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Anthropic quietly reset Claude Code's 5-hour and weekly usage limits for every paying user on July 10, 2026. ClaudeDevs, the company's official Claude Code updates account, announced the reset, which applied to Pro, Max, Team, and seat-based Enterprise plans. It is the latest in a string of quota resets following a rough stretch of reliability incidents — elevated errors across claude.ai, the Console, the API, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork on July 3; an OAuth login failure on July 6 that blocked Claude Code sign-ins; and roughly two hours of degraded performance across Claude Code on the web, Code Review, Cowork Remote, Claude Tag, and document creation on July 8.

The reset lands three days before a temporary 50% weekly limit boost is set to expire. That boost went live on May 13, widely read at the time as Anthropic's answer to OpenAI's Codex pricing, and covers the same Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise tiers now getting the fresh quota. The overlap suggests the company is trying to keep power users whole through a stretch where the capacity plan and the growth plan are colliding — the Claude Cowork rollout to mobile and web on July 8 added a large new load on the same shared model backend that Claude Code already leans on.

This is the industry-wide shape of scaling: the model demo works, the pricing works, and then the reliability curve bends when a second surface goes live on the same infrastructure. Anthropic has publicly acknowledged the incidents on its status page rather than papering over them, and the resets buy goodwill with the developers who feel the cap the hardest — but three resets in seven days is not sustainable messaging for enterprise buyers who need a predictable rate limit to plan against.

For learners: reliability is a feature, and it's the one users notice first. If you are building on a hosted model, always design for graceful degradation — retry with backoff on 5xx errors, fall back to a smaller or alternate model when the primary is down, and never let a single provider be the only path to a critical workflow. The uptime numbers you read on a status page look small until they are your Tuesday afternoon deadline.
